bins.name: Pacing mismatch study need
bins.desc: The filer identifies a coordination failure: data centers deploy in 18 to 24 months while grid infrastructure takes 7 to 10 years. This structural lag is the foundation for recommending a federal framework rather than leaving the problem to case-by-case interconnection.
quotes: The rapid growth of AI data center load presents a critical coordination challenge due to the temporal mismatch between rapid data center deployment (18–24 months) and slow grid infrastructure expansion (7–10 years).

bins.name: Treat flexibility as system value
bins.desc: The filer argues that data centers hold significant latent flexibility through workload shifting, UPS response, and on-site generation, and that current rules ignore this. She supports modernizing market participation rules so these capabilities reduce upgrade costs and system stress.
quotes: Modern data centers possess significant latent flexibility through workload shifting, UPS-based frequency response, and hybrid on-site generation.
Current interconnection rules treat these loads as static and inflexible, ignoring capabilities that could significantly reduce system stress and upgrade costs.

The Commission should establish a federal framework that lowers barriers to flexible resources, modernizes market participation rules for technologies like thermal energy storage (TES), and reforms cost allocation to reflect marginal impacts.

bins.name: Reform upgrade cost allocation
bins.desc: The filer opposes 100% participant funding as grounded in flawed embedded-cost methodology that ignores marginal impacts. She advocates Ramsey-Boiteux cost allocation, warning that full cost assignment to elastic large loads will push them toward grid defection.
quotes: Current interconnection rules treat these loads as static and inflexible, ignoring capabilities that could significantly reduce system stress and upgrade costs.
Legacy interconnection rules create high asset specificity and hold-up risks, driving developers toward inefficient bypass strategies.
The ANOPR's suggestion of 100% participant funding for upgrades relies on flawed embedded-cost methodologies that ignore marginal impacts.
Efficient cost recovery should instead follow Ramsey-Boiteux principles, allocating fixed costs inversely to elasticity.
Because large loads are highly elastic and can relocate or self-supply, assigning them full upgrade costs encourages inefficient grid defection.

The Commission should establish a federal framework that lowers barriers to flexible resources, modernizes market participation rules for technologies like thermal energy storage (TES), and reforms cost allocation to reflect marginal impacts.

bins.name: Facilitate co-location economically
bins.desc: The filer frames co-location as a rational make-vs-buy response to high transaction costs in legacy interconnection rules. She supports extending generator-style interconnection regimes to loads rather than penalizing co-location arrangements.
quotes: Co-location and hybrid configurations are rational economic responses to these high transaction costs ("make vs. buy" decisions) and should be facilitated rather than penalized by extending generator-style interconnection regimes to loads.

bins.name: Elastic loads and grid defection risk
bins.desc: The filer notes that large loads are highly elastic and can relocate or self-supply, making full upgrade cost assignment counterproductive. This frames the proximate-load question in terms of economic efficiency rather than geography.
quotes: Because large loads are highly elastic and can relocate or self-supply, assigning them full upgrade costs encourages inefficient grid defection.

bins.name: Participant funding methodology flawed
bins.desc: The filer opposes the ANOPR's suggestion of 100% participant funding, arguing it relies on embedded-cost methodologies that ignore marginal cost impacts and misalign incentives for large elastic loads.
quotes: The ANOPR's suggestion of 100% participant funding for upgrades relies on flawed embedded-cost methodologies that ignore marginal impacts.

bins.name: Deployment vs. grid buildout lag
bins.desc: The structural mismatch between fast data center deployment and slow grid expansion is the organizing problem the filing addresses. The filer uses it to justify a modular, principles-based federal response.
quotes: The rapid growth of AI data center load presents a critical coordination challenge due to the temporal mismatch between rapid data center deployment (18–24 months) and slow grid infrastructure expansion (7–10 years).

bins.name: Undervalued data center flexibility
bins.desc: The filer argues current rules treat large loads as static, overlooking workload shifting, UPS frequency response, and hybrid generation. Recognizing and enabling this flexibility could materially reduce infrastructure costs.
quotes: Modern data centers possess significant latent flexibility through workload shifting, UPS-based frequency response, and hybrid on-site generation.
Current interconnection rules treat these loads as static and inflexible, ignoring capabilities that could significantly reduce system stress and upgrade costs.

bins.name: Legacy rules raise transaction costs
bins.desc: Applying transaction-cost economics, the filer argues that legacy interconnection rules create asset specificity and hold-up risks that push developers toward bypass strategies. Co-location is a rational economic response, not an abuse.
quotes: Legacy interconnection rules create high asset specificity and hold-up risks, driving developers toward inefficient bypass strategies.

Co-location and hybrid configurations are rational economic responses to these high transaction costs ("make vs. buy" decisions) and should be facilitated rather than penalized by extending generator-style interconnection regimes to loads.

bins.name: Marginal vs. embedded cost principles
bins.desc: The filer argues cost allocation should follow Ramsey-Boiteux principles rather than embedded-cost averages. Assigning full upgrade costs to highly elastic loads encourages grid defection and is economically inefficient.
quotes: Efficient cost recovery should instead follow Ramsey-Boiteux principles, allocating fixed costs inversely to elasticity.
Because large loads are highly elastic and can relocate or self-supply, assigning them full upgrade costs encourages inefficient grid defection.

bins.name: Market rules for flexible technologies
bins.desc: The filer calls for modernized market participation rules covering technologies like thermal energy storage so that demand-side flexibility can be valued and compensated within wholesale markets.
quotes: The Commission should establish a federal framework that lowers barriers to flexible resources, modernizes market participation rules for technologies like thermal energy storage (TES), and reforms cost allocation to reflect marginal impacts.
